rolloutContext.note: 100% rollout went live 2026-05-07. Refreshed 2026-06-08 (Monday). Sample 4,412 devices on the NEW slice. CAVEAT: NEW-device split holds at 53.7/46.3 (2,369 A / 2,043 B), chi-sq p<0.0001 — the sliding-window artifact (dormant pre-experiment devices misclassified as NEW, dragging Flow A's sticky bucket), not a fresh SRM. Anchor the cohort before quoting split-sensitive metrics. Completion gap +2.7pp B (trend: +14 → +8.7 → +6.7 → +3.6 → +2.9 → +3.1 → +2.8 → +2.6 → +2.7 — converged and flat). Login success rate tied/slightly A (−0.5pp). B's durable wins: welcome reach (+6.5pp) and forgot-password friction (−25.1pp, held). Login abandons gap CLOSED/flipped — now A 53.1 / B 52.6 per 100 devices (B slightly lower). Per-exposure ~tied (A 6.6 / B 7.0). WATCH: scope leak crept 7 → 8; duplicate rate elevated but softening (20.2 / 18.8 / 16.4%). CS CORRECTED: fresh export (47 accidental-indy cancellations since Apr 1) shows accidental signups steady ~3-5/wk by signup week, NO May 18 spike (that was inc-264 abuse caught by the Snowflake proxy); the experiment is flat on accidental signups and does NOT win on that metric — it wins on friction, welcome reach, and catching duplicates at the email step.

abandonFollowup.headline: 80% of Flow B email_entry abandoners come back — concern is essentially resolved.
abandonFollowup.subtitle: Original concern was Flow B's high login_abandoned rate vs Flow A's. We traced each abandoner's anonymous_id to see if they returned.
abandonFollowup.method: Took all distinct ANONYMOUS_IDs that fired login_abandoned with last_step_reached='email_entry' in Flow B (NEW devices, post-100%). For each, checked whether they had any LOGIN_SUCCEEDED, ACCOUNT_CREATED, or IDENTIFIES event AFTER their first abandon timestamp.
abandonFollowup.implicationHeadline: Most 'abandonment' is users retrying. Truly-lost share holds around 1 in 5.
implicationBullets: 66% (148/224) completed login or signup after abandoning.
Return rate 81% — the email-step concern is essentially resolved.
abandonFollowup.implicationWhy: Flow B has more measurable abandon surfaces than Flow A, which inflates the raw rate.
abandonFollowup.verdict: Concern essentially resolved. 80% of abandoners return. Session recordings (Clarity is deployed) should clarify what the rest do — check with Tristan.
abandonFollowup.caveat: Sample 224 abandoners. Refreshed 2026-06-08. The 48 'came back but no outcome' bucket may complete later.

indieClassification.headline: Zero indie ACCOUNT_CREATED in the experiment population. The 'accidental indies' question now lives in the Historical comparison block.
indieClassification.definition: An indie is identified by CONTEXT_TRAITS_ROLE = 'barber' AND CONTEXT_TRAITS_USER_TYPE IS NULL in the IDENTIFIES table. Rental and Commission are explicit user_type values used for shop-employed barbers; indies have no shop relationship and therefore no user_type set.

method.headline: Used ever-had trait logic across IDENTIFIES to avoid transient null user_type.
bullets: Cross-joined ACCOUNT_CREATED users with IDENTIFIES traits at any timestamp.
MAX_BY produced misleading nulls because user_type is transient at re-identify.
Population reflects ACCOUNT_CREATED in the experiment, not the historical proxy.

tempPasswordCorrection.reality: All Flow B email_check temp_password results map to confirmed Rental/Commission barbers — they get temp_password from their shop owner. Indies do not show up via temp_password.

impactOnNarrative.headline: temp_password catches invited shop barbers, not indies. Rename the signal.
bullets: 33.5% temp_password share is the strongest Flow B signal.
It prevents duplicate accounts from already-provisioned shop barbers.
Stop describing it as 'catching indies'. Those barbers are Rental or Commission.

interpretation.headline: Indies exist at scale, but ACCOUNT_CREATED has no pre-experiment baseline.
bullets: 1,641 indies active in IDENTIFIES over the last 90 days.
ACCOUNT_CREATED was instrumented at experiment deploy, so no historical reference.
IDENTIFIES first-seen powers the Historical comparison block as a usable proxy.

implications: North-star metric 'reduce accidental indies in Flow B' cannot be measured: zero indies created accounts in this traffic.
Likely cause: indies sign up via web or a separate self-onboarding flow, not iOS/Android login.
Ask Eric/Yurii if indie signups historically come through iOS/Android. If not, we need a new success metric.

cumulative.label: Cumulative · Apr 30 → May 12
cumulative.refreshNote: Numbers in this block are from Friday's pull (May 8). Refresh of cumulative not strictly needed for the new Monday narrative since the post-100% NEW window is now the canonical lens; cumulative is preserved as 'broad operational view'. Re-pull next session if needed.

northStarData.note: flowB barber/signup_link = 1 user (instrumentation leak from PR #4143 not yet merged)

postRollout.label: Post-100% rollout · May 7 → May 12
postRollout.refreshNote: Refreshed 2026-05-12. Sample 4x bigger than Friday read.

variantBalance.srmStatus: 67.7/32.3 — drifting toward 50/50 as the legacy pool dilutes. Still contaminated by sticky bucketing of pre-experiment devices.

northStarData.note: Still 0 indie in either variant; flowB barber/signup_link leak still active (1 user)

postRolloutNew.label: Post-100% NEW devices only · cleanest experiment read
postRolloutNew.filterDescription: Devices whose first IDENTIFIES event is on or after 2026-05-07 — i.e., genuinely new entries to the experiment, not pre-existing legacy users with sticky LD buckets.
postRolloutNew.refreshNote: Refreshed 2026-06-08 (Monday). Sample 4,412 devices. CAVEAT: split holds at 53.7/46.3 (2,369 A / 2,043 B), chi-sq p<0.0001 — sliding-window artifact (dormant pre-experiment devices misclassified as NEW), not a fresh SRM. Completion gap +2.7pp B (trend: ...+3.1 → +2.8 → +2.6 → +2.7 — converged and flat). Login success tied/slightly A (48.9% A vs 48.4% B). B's wins: welcome reach (+6.5pp) and forgot-password (−25.1pp, held). Login abandons gap CLOSED/flipped — A 53.1 vs B 52.6 per 100 (B now slightly lower). Per-exposure ~tied (A 6.6 vs B 7.0). WATCH: scope leak crept 7 → 8; duplicates elevated but softening (20.2 / 18.8 / 16.4%). CS corrected: accidental signups steady ~3-5/wk by signup week, experiment flat on that metric.

variantBalance.srmStatus: 53.7/46.3 — drift essentially held (50.8/49.2 → 52.6/47.4 → 53.8/46.2 → 53.6/46.4 → 53.7/46.3). chi-sq p<0.0001. The monotonic climb as the 90-day window slides confirms the method artifact: dormant pre-experiment devices that return after May 7 land their first in-window IDENTIFIES post-May-7, get misclassified as NEW, and carry the legacy Flow-A sticky bucket. A fixed (anchored) cohort would not drift like this. Anchor the cohort (first-EVER identify >= May 7) before quoting split-sensitive metrics; within-flow rates (friction, funnel per-flow) remain valid.

funnel.note: Flow B welcome > assigned because some devices were assigned during the ramp but their first IDENTIFIES event is May 7+. Trivial data-quirk. Conversion rate (succeeded ÷ welcome) is tied/marginally A. B's overall edge comes entirely from welcome reach.

northStarData.note: On clean sample, 0 indie account_created. Conclusion that indies don't pass through this channel holds. ⚠ Flow B scope leak (barber/signup_link) crept to 8 devices (3 → 7 → 8). Version breakdown still not re-pulled — ask iOS whether these are stale 3.23.0 installs or a 3.23.1 regression.

scopeLeak.fixStatus: PR #4143 merged for 3.23.1 — leak crept to 8 (3 → 7 → 8 across refreshes), re-check with iOS
scopeLeak.note: ⚠ Leak crept to 8 Flow B barber/signup_link users (3 → 7 → 8 across the last three refreshes). Count here is ACCOUNT_CREATED-based; per-version breakdown not re-pulled — ask iOS whether these are stale 3.23.0 installs or a 3.23.1 regression.

srmInvestigation.headline: SRM 73/27 root-caused: sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. New devices post-100% split 59/41, the 'true' experiment SRM.

byDay.takeaway: Split is stable across all post-100% days (72-74/26-32) in the 'all devices' view. The cumulative 73/27 we see is the dilution of that mix. NEW-only filter shows the true experiment-grade 50.7/49.3.

byAppVersion.takeaway: Post-100% has zero legacy version contamination — every device is on 3.23.0. The SRM is NOT explained by 'old versions only emit flowA'.

byFirstSeen.headline: ROOT CAUSE: device-age stratification reveals sticky-bucket effect

rows.interpretation: Devices that arrived during low rollout

takeaway.headline: Sticky LaunchDarkly buckets, not a bug, drive the 73/27 SRM.
bullets: Pre-experiment devices skew 80/20 Flow A (194 vs 48), stuck on the 1% default.
Ramp-window devices split 77/23 (17 vs 5), similar legacy inertia.
Post-100% NEW devices split 59/41 (77 vs 54), close to expected 50/50.
LD never re-buckets per anonymous_id, so the legacy pool carries the skew.
takeaway.why: Confirms the cumulative SRM is mechanical, not a flaw. Experiment reads stay valid.

iosFiringContext.headline: iOS code review: LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires from AuthorizationFlowCoordinator.start()

takeaway.headline: Assignment fires only at auth start, which explains Flow A's logged-in skew.
bullets: LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires from AuthorizationFlowCoordinator.start(), not every app open.
Logged-in users trigger it via barber/shop context switch, landing on Flow A.
New devices trigger it on cold start and land on Flow B.
Explains Flow A's 56% logged-in rate vs Flow B's 3%.
takeaway.why: Rules out a bucketing bug. Login-rate gap is mechanical, consistent with device-age stratification.

loginRate.takeaway: Direct consequence of the device-age stratification: flowA is 67% pre-experiment devices (with login history); flowB is 50% new devices (no login history yet).

tempPasswordInvestigation.headline: Steady-state catch rate is ~27% on clean NEW data, down from 35% during the early backlog.

takeaway.headline: Steady-state catch is ~27% on clean NEW data, down from 35% backlog peak.
bullets: Two effects layered: legacy contamination dilutes the all-post-100% view to 23%.
Invited-barber backlog softened: 36.9% ramp to 35% NEW week 1 to 27% NEW refresh.
Clean NEW-only data is the right lens, not the contaminated all-devices view.
Flow B still catches roughly 1 in 4 email checks as an invited barber.

inputTypeMix.takeaway: Username share rose slightly (7.9% → 11.0%) but is not the cause of the apparent drop — sample contamination is.

notes.tldr: 73/27 is real but caused by sticky LD bucketing — not a bug.
bullets: Pre-experiment devices skew 80/20 flowA-heavy (legacy pool)
Post-100% NEW devices: 59/41 — much closer to 50/50
NEW finding: flowB only 3% logged-in vs flowA 56%
Mechanical, not a bucketing flaw — see SRM investigation

notes.tldr: Flow B converts ~9 pp better than Flow A across all windows.
bullets: Week 1 ramp: B 67.5% vs A 58.7%
Cumulative: B 67.2% vs A 58.6%
Gap holds — not a ramp artifact

notes.tldr: No real drop. Apparent 23% was sample contamination — NEW-only is steady at 35%, like the ramp.
bullets: Ramp: 36.9% (clean baseline)
Post-100% all (contaminated): 23.0% — diluted by legacy 'returning' events
Post-100% NEW only (clean): 35.1% — back to baseline
Steady-state catch rate of invited barbers: ~35%

notes.tldr: Flow B has more barber signups via signup path — direction we'd want reversed.
bullets: Both variants dropped uniformly week-over-week
Flow B still ~2× Flow A (gap persists)
Could be intent we can't see (legit new barbers vs invited)
Session recordings can help disambiguate (Clarity is deployed — review with Tristan)

notes.tldr: Frustration tapping is real (12-24 taps per affected user) and persistent.
bullets: Ramp: 24.3 taps per affected user
Post-100%: 12.4 taps per affected user
Concentrated in 5-9 users per period (not widespread)
Non-dismissible Flow A sheet → escape attempt

notes.metric: Login abandoned at email_entry (Flow B new step)
notes.tldr: Flow B's new email_entry step accumulates abandonment as volume grows.
bullets: Week 1: 57 events
Cumulative: 111 events
Post-100%: 31 events (aligned with daily pace)
Session recordings are now available (Clarity is deployed) — review with Tristan

notes.tldr: Leak flat at 3 devices since yesterday — fix may not have rolled out enough.
bullets: Week 1: 5 events / 3 devices
Cumulative: 8 events / 6 devices
May 13 (NEW only): 1 event / 1 device
May 18 (NEW only): 3 events / 3 devices
May 19 (NEW only): 3 events / 3 devices — flat, not declining yet

indieChannelInvestigation.headline: 97% of new indies don't pass through the iOS auth flow — the experiment cannot measure the indie north-star

takeaway.headline: Only 2 of 69 new indies pass through the experiment. The channel is wrong.
bullets: 67 of 69 new indies signed up via a separate flow entirely.
Indie north-star cannot be measured directly from this experiment population.
Historical accidental-indies block is now the canonical read on duplicate prevention.
takeaway.why: The success metric must be redefined or measured via a different instrumentation path.

separateChannelFinding.headline: Indies have their own self-onboarding flow — completely separate from the login experiment
separateChannelFinding.evidence: RUDDER_EVENTS schema contains a family of *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events (TIKTOK_APPEARED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, VIDEO_ADDED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, WORK_HOURS_SAVED_IN_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, X_ADDED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, etc.). This is a distinct onboarding path the experiment does not touch.

implication.headline: Indies sign up via a separate product surface the experiment cannot touch.
bullets: Rudder has a family of *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events used by that flow.
iOS login auth flow does not route indies, so the north-star is unmeasurable here.
To measure indie signups, instrument the self-onboarding flow or query its tables.

forgotPasswordInvestigation.headline: Frustration tapping pattern persists post-100% — not a legacy artifact, real UX issue

takeaway.headline: Frustration tapping is a real UX issue, not a legacy artifact.
bullets: Ramp: 24.3 taps per affected user across 9 users.
Post-100%: 12.4 taps per affected user across 5 users.
Pattern persists across all app versions and rollout phases.
Concentrated escape attempts on the non-dismissible Flow A sheet.
Session recordings now in place (Clarity deployed) — review post-tap behavior with Tristan to confirm the hypothesis.
takeaway.why: Drives the call on whether to make the Flow A sheet dismissible or add a clearer exit.

conclusions.question: Why is the SRM 73/27?
conclusions.tldr: Sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout — not a bucketing bug.
bullets: Pre-experiment devices: 80/20 flowA-heavy (legacy pool stuck on default)
Post-100% NEW devices: 59/41 — close to expected 50/50
LD assigns once per device and doesn't re-bucket on rollout changes
Cumulative SRM is dominated by the legacy pool; 'true' SRM is closer to 60/40
conclusions.answer: Sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. Pre-experiment devices skew 80/20 flowA-heavy because LD started at 1% and most old devices stayed on the default. New devices post-100% split 59/41 — closer to expected.
conclusions.evidence: byFirstSeen breakdown: 194/48 pre-experiment vs 77/54 post-100% NEW devices.

conclusions.question: Why does flowB have only 3% logged-in rate vs flowA's 56%?
conclusions.tldr: Mechanical effect of when the iOS auth flow fires — not a bucketing bug.
bullets: LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires only at AuthorizationFlowCoordinator.start()
Already-logged-in users hit it via 'switchToExperience' (barber↔shop)
New devices hit it on cold-start (not yet logged in)
flowA captures more context-switching existing users (pre-experiment pool)
conclusions.answer: iOS code review confirms LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires only when AuthorizationFlowCoordinator starts. Already-logged-in users hit it via context-switch; new flowB devices hit it on first launch.

conclusions.question: Are temp_password users indies?
conclusions.tldr: No — they're all shop-employed barbers (Rental or Commission).
bullets: 0 of 95 new barbers in the experiment are indies
All have user_type set to Rental or Commission
Indies are barbers WITHOUT user_type set (no shop relationship)
Tristan's hypothesis was right — temp_password = invited shop barber
conclusions.answer: No — all temp_password users in Flow B are confirmed Rental or Commission (shop-employed barbers). Zero indies created accounts in the experiment.
conclusions.evidence: Cross-joined 95 experiment ACCOUNT_CREATED users with IDENTIFIES traits — 100% Rental/Commission.

conclusions.question: Can we measure the indie north-star in this experiment?
conclusions.tldr: No — indies have their own onboarding flow, separate from the iOS auth flow.
bullets: Only 2 of 69 new indies pass through LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED
97% of new indies sign up via a different path entirely
Rudder has *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events — a separate product surface
The experiment's success metric must be redefined
conclusions.answer: No — indies have their own self-onboarding flow (*_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events). The iOS login auth flow doesn't touch that path.
conclusions.evidence: JOIN of new-indie ANONYMOUS_IDs with LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED: 2/69 overlap + existence of *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING tables.

conclusions.question: Why did temp_password share appear to drop from 33.5% to 23%?
conclusions.tldr: Two effects: sample contamination + real backlog softening. Steady-state on clean NEW data is ~27%.
bullets: Ramp baseline: 36.9% (early invited-barber backlog catching up)
All post-100% (contaminated): 23.0% — diluted by legacy 'returning'
NEW post-100% week 1 (5/8): 35.1% — early backlog still present
NEW post-100% refresh (5/11): 26.7% — steady-state
Even at 27%, that's ~1 in 4 email checks catching an invited barber — meaningful
conclusions.answer: Two layered effects. The all-post-100% view is contaminated by pre-experiment legacy users who fire mostly 'returning' (diluting the share). And within the clean NEW-only sample, the first days captured residual invited-barber backlog and then softened to ~27% steady-state.
conclusions.evidence: Email check NEW-only refreshed Monday 5/11: 74 events / 8 users / 26.7%. Compared to Friday NEW-only: 26 events / 4 users / 35.1%.

conclusions.question: Is the Flow A frustration tapping a legacy artifact?
conclusions.tldr: No — the pattern persists across windows. Real UX issue.
bullets: Ramp: 24.3 taps per affected user (9 users)
Post-100%: 12.4 taps per affected user (5 users)
Concentrated in a small number — clearly an escape attempt
Session recordings now available (Clarity deployed) — review post-tap behavior with Tristan
conclusions.answer: No — the pattern persists in post-100% (12.4 taps/user) and ramp (24.3 taps/user). It's a real UX issue with the non-dismissible sheet.
conclusions.evidence: FORGOT_PASSWORD_TAPPED Flow A · flow_a_sheet · both windows.

openQuestions: Clarity is deployed on iOS. First session recordings are available — review with Tristan to confirm the 'escape attempt' hypothesis on forgot_password and the email-step abandon behavior.

scorecard.headline: Refreshed 2026-06-08 (Monday). CAVEAT: NEW-device split holds 53.7/46.3 (p<0.0001) — confirmed sliding-window artifact; anchor the cohort before quoting split-sensitive reads. Completion gap +2.7pp B (converged, flat 5 refreshes). Login success tied/slightly A. B's wins: welcome reach (+6.5pp), forgot-password (−25.1pp, held). Abandons gap closed/flipped (B now slightly lower). Watch-items: scope leak crept to 8, duplicates elevated but softening (20.2/18.8/16.4%). CS CORRECTED: accidental signups steady ~3-5/wk by signup week, experiment flat on that metric.
scorecard.sourceWindow: post-100% NEW devices only · May 7 - Jun 8

rows.definition: Share of users assigned to each flow. Should be even.
rows.note: ⚠ Drifted then essentially held as the window slid (50.8/49.2 → 52.6/47.4 → 53.8/46.2 → 53.6/46.4 → 53.7/46.3, p<0.0001) — that pattern CONFIRMS the method artifact (dormant old devices misclassified as NEW), not a fresh bucketing bug. Within-flow rates below are unaffected; fix is to anchor the cohort.

rows.definition: Share of assigned users who reached the welcome screen, per flow.
rows.note: † Flow B shows >100% from an innocuous ramp-window quirk. Email-first sheet auto-loads — this is B's most durable advantage and the source of its remaining completion edge.

rows.metric: Login success rate (of users who saw welcome)
rows.definition: Share of welcome-viewers who completed login, per flow.
rows.note: Tied/marginally A. Trend: +10 → +4 → +3 → 0 → −0.6 → −0.3 → −0.4 → −0.5 → −0.5pp. Once at the welcome screen, both flows convert the same. B's overall edge is entirely the welcome-reach delta.

rows.definition: End-to-end conversion through the whole funnel, per flow.
rows.note: Trend: +14 → +8.7 → +6.7 → +3.6 → +2.9 → +3.1 → +2.8 → +2.6 → +2.7pp. Converged and flat for five refreshes (~+3pp). B still ahead but the margin is small and driven by welcome reach.

rows.definition: Forgot-password tap events per device, per flow.
rows.note: B's strongest win, and it kept widening then held (−19.6 → −24.6 → −25.1 → −25.1pp). Flow A's non-dismissible sheet drives frustration tapping; pairs with the misclick analysis in deck 27 (A misclick rate 36% vs B 10%).

rows.definition: Login-abandon events ÷ devices × 100, per flow. Each device can fire multiple.
rows.note: * The gap closed and flipped (+4.7 → +2.4 → +1.7 → −0.5): Flow B is now marginally LOWER than A. B has 2 abandon surfaces (email + password), A has 1, so this is a genuine improvement, not just mechanical. Followup: 81% of B abandoners return later anyway.

rows.definition: New barber accounts created via signup path ÷ devices, per flow.
rows.direction: lower better (we'd rather existing barbers log in than create duplicates)
rows.note: * Roughly tied; B ticked up to 7.0 vs A 6.6 (gap +0.4pp), still inside the noise band that's held all experiment. Lower is better (fewer duplicate signups); A marginally lower.

rows.metric: Caught invited barbers before duplicate signup (B only)
rows.definition: Share of email checks that match an existing invited barber, caught before they create a duplicate.
rows.note: Steady around 21%. Flow B catches ~1 in 5 invited barbers — a capability Flow A doesn't have at all.

rows.definition: Accidental indy self-signups. Snowflake proxy = indies whose email matches a Rental/Commission account; CS ground-truth = cancellations tagged 'Accidental Indy Self-Signup', counted by signup week.
rows.note: CORRECTED with the fresh CS export (47 cancellations since Apr 1). By SIGNUP week, accidental signups are steady ~3-5/wk before and during the experiment (May 4/11/18 = 3/3/4, no spike), so the experiment is FLAT on this metric — it does NOT win here. The old '0 in May' was a premature partial pull and is dead. The Snowflake proxy's mid-May 'spike' (peak 12-13) was the inc-264 abuse incident, not real accidental signups, and the proxy over-counts anyway (~50% are legit barber-to-shop transitions). Lead the Flow B case on friction + welcome reach + duplicate-catch, not this.

verdict.headline: Flow B still the call, on the narrower grounds: welcome reach + forgot-password friction + duplicate-catch. Conversion converged and flat for 5 refreshes (~+3pp). It does NOT win on accidental signups (flat).
bullets: Flow B's durable wins: welcome reach (+6.5pp), forgot-password friction (−25.1pp, held — pairs with the misclick analysis in deck 27), and it catches ~1 in 5 invited barbers before a duplicate (Flow A can't).

CS CORRECTED: fresh export shows 47 accidental-indy cancellations since Apr 1, steady ~3-5/wk by signup week (May 4/11/18 = 3/3/4, no spike). The experiment is FLAT on accidental signups — drop the old '0 in May' claim (premature partial pull). Don't overclaim an accidental-signup reduction.
Completion gap +2.7pp (from +14pp) — converged and flat. Login success tied/slightly A. Login abandons gap closed/flipped (B now slightly lower).
caveats: NEW-device split holds at 53.7/46.3 (p<0.0001), drifted then held as the window slid — confirmed sliding-window artifact, not a fresh SRM. Anchor the cohort definition (first-EVER identify >= May 7) before quoting split-sensitive metrics.
The conversion-win narrative from week 1 has evaporated. Lead with friction reduction + duplicate-catch, not completion.

Accidental signups: the experiment is flat. The Snowflake proxy is noisy and over-counts (~50% legit transitions); its mid-May 'spike' was the inc-264 abuse incident. CS ground-truth (steady ~3-5/wk) is the read to present.
Login abandons: gap closed and flipped — A 53.1 vs B 52.6 per 100. 81% of B abandoners return.
⚠ Scope leak crept to 8 devices (3 → 7 → 8) — re-check versions with iOS.
⚠ Duplicate-email rate elevated but softening (20.2 / 18.8 / 16.4% vs ~15.7% baseline) — trending back toward baseline; keep watching.

historicalComparison.headline: CS export now in hand (47 accidental-indy self-signup cancellations since Apr 1). By signup week, accidental signups are steady ~3-5/wk before and during the experiment — the experiment is FLAT on this metric, not a win. The old '0 in May' was a premature partial pull. The Snowflake proxy's mid-May 'spike' was the inc-264 abuse incident, not real signups.

statusNote.headline: Baseline 16 weeks (Jan 5 → Apr 26). Snowflake proxy is noisy and over-counts; CS ground-truth (now refreshed) is the read.
bullets: Pre-experiment baseline covers full year-to-date (16 weeks), frozen (out of the rolling 90-day window).
Accidental-indies block is the in-data proxy, but it reclassifies as weeks mature and over-counts (~50% are legit barber-to-shop transitions). The CS export is the ground-truth.

Experiment-window blocks refreshed 2026-06-08. CS re-pull DONE — fresh export as of 2026-06-03 14:32 EST (47 records since Apr 1, filtered to Internal Reason = Accidental Indy Self-Signup). Honest read: accidental signups are flat, the experiment does not move them.

blocks.title: ★ Accidental indies · weekly (sharpest signal)
blocks.subtitle: Indies whose email ALSO belongs to a Rental/Commission account — strong proxy for 'accidental duplicate signups'. This is what the new flow should specifically reduce.

experiment.label: Experiment (Apr 27 - Jun 1, 6 full weeks)
experiment.value: 8.3/wk avg proxy · but CS ground-truth shows accidental signups FLAT at ~3-5/wk by signup week

verdict.headline: CORRECTED: the experiment is FLAT on accidental signups, not a win. The CS export (ground-truth) shows steady ~3-5/wk by signup week before and during the experiment. The Snowflake proxy is noisy, over-counts (~50% legit transitions), and its mid-May 'spike' was the inc-264 abuse incident.
bullets: CS ground-truth (47 cancellations since Apr 1): by signup week, accidental signups are steady ~3-5/wk. Experiment weeks May 4/11/18 = 3/3/4 — no spike, no drop. FLAT.
The old '0 cancellations in May' was a premature partial pull (May 11) and is dead — real number is not zero (~11 with May signup dates so far).
The Snowflake proxy's mid-May rise (peak 12-13 accidental) was the inc-264 abuse incident (fraudulent free-trial accounts ~May 15), not real accidental signups.
Proxy over-counts: ~50% of 'accidental indies' are likely legit barber-to-shop transitions (see shop-tenure breakdown below).
Proxy weeks (accidental) shown below for transparency: 2 / 8 / 13 / 12 / 7 / 8 across Apr 27 - Jun 1.
Pre-experiment baseline stable over 16 weeks (range 7-23), so the early proxy 'drop' was reclassification noise, not a real trend.
verdict.why: The proxy overstated a drop that never existed in the ground-truth. Don't claim Flow B reduces accidental signups; it wins on friction, welcome reach, and catching duplicates at the email step.

weeks.note: abuse-incident week (inc-264); proxy reclassifies across refreshes. The mid-May rise was fraud accounts, not real accidental signups — CS by signup week shows only 4 that week
full week; settled to 7 accidental after the incident

blocks.caveat: Refreshed 2026-06-08. Experiment weeks (Apr 27+) re-pulled; the Jan 5 - Apr 26 baseline is frozen (those weeks have fallen out of the rolling 90-day query window, so they're preserved from earlier pulls). Method: take all 'new indies' (role='barber', final user_type stayed null), cross-check whether their email also exists for any Rental/Commission user_id. Match = likely accidental. NOTE: this proxy reclassifies as weeks mature AND over-counts (~50% are legit barber-to-shop transitions). The CS export by signup week (steady ~3-5/wk) is the ground-truth and shows the experiment is FLAT on accidental signups.

shopTenureBreakdown.title: Refinement · shop tenure of accidental indies (90d cohort)

rows.bucket: Same day or earlier than shop account
rows.label: Likely legit transition (had indie biz, then joined a shop)
Likely accidental (downloaded app much later, signed up wrong)

takeaway.headline: Half of 'accidental indies' are likely legitimate barber-to-shop transitions.
bullets: 50% (100 of 201) created the indie account same-day or before the shop account.
True accidentals (indie after shop invitation) total ~101 of 201 in 90 days.
Drop signal still holds on the narrower cohort.
Absolute baseline is roughly half of the headline number.
takeaway.why: Tightens the definition without invalidating the directional Flow B win.

blocks.title: New indie signups · weekly (total volume)
blocks.subtitle: Indies (final user_type stayed NULL) first appearing in IDENTIFIES, weekly. Drop observed during experiment weeks but the inference 'this is the new flow preventing accidental signups' is NOT yet fully established — accidental-indies block is the sharper signal.

verdict.headline: Total indies down ~26% (eroded from the early -47% read), causation not established — and accidental signups (the sharper question) are flat per CS.
bullets: 19.5/wk to 14.3/wk average vs the 16-week pre-experiment baseline (8+10+15+24+13+16 across 6 full weeks).
Volume was already trending down pre-experiment, so some drop is organic; it also rose mid-May (inc-264 abuse incident) then settled.
The ★ Accidental-indies block and the CS export are the reads to use, not total-indie volume.
CS export (refreshed): accidental signups are steady ~3-5/wk by signup week — the experiment is FLAT on accidental signups.

weeks.note: mid-May rise coincides with the inc-264 abuse incident, not real accidental signups (CS shows 4 accidental by signup that week)

blocks.caveat: Refreshed 2026-06-08. Experiment weeks re-pulled; pre-experiment baseline frozen (out of the 90-day window). Pre-experiment had a mild declining trend. The early '-47%' read eroded as weeks matured — indie volume rose mid-May (inc-264 abuse incident) then settled (15 → 24 → 13 → 16/wk). Total-indie volume is not the signal; CS accidental signups (flat ~3-5/wk) is.

blocks.title: Duplicate barber-account-per-email rate
blocks.subtitle: % of new barber USER_IDs whose email already belongs to another USER_ID. Most direct prevention signal the new flow should move.

comparison.verdict: Roughly at baseline (16.0% vs 15.7%). Ran hot mid-May (20.2 / 18.8%) but is now softening back toward baseline (16.4% the week of Jun 1). No duplicate-prevention signal in this metric. Not the place to claim a Flow B win; lean on the email-step catch (~21%) + the friction wins instead, and keep watching the recent softening.

weeks.note: ran hot at 20.2% (vs ~15% baseline) — overlaps the inc-264 abuse incident
now a complete week; softening to 16.4%

blocks.caveat: Older weeks under-count duplicates because we don't have history before the 90-day window. Refreshed 2026-06-08. Pre-experiment baseline 15.7% avg; experiment 6 full weeks 16.0%. The mid-May spike (20.2 / 18.8%) is softening back toward baseline (16.4% the week of Jun 1) — consistent with the inc-264 abuse incident driving it. Keep watching.

blocks.title: New barber breakdown · Rental / Commission / Indie weekly
blocks.subtitle: Total new barbers per week segmented by final user_type. The mix shifted slightly toward shop-employed during experiment weeks.

experiment.label: Experiment indie share (Apr 27 - Jun 1, 6 full weeks)

comparison.verdict: Honest update: the early 'indie share dropped' read INVERTED as weeks matured — share is now 3.2% vs 2.8% baseline, lifted by the mid-May inc-264 abuse incident (5.6% the week of May 18). This metric does not support a new-flow effect; the ★ Accidental indies block and the CS export are the signals to use.

blocks.title: Active users base · weekly IDENTIFIES
blocks.subtitle: Total distinct devices in IDENTIFIES per week. Important denominator: if user base shrank during experiment, signup drops could be 'fewer users' rather than 'the new flow working'.

comparison.verdict: User base grew ~21% during the experiment (850k to 1.03M devices/wk, peaking ~1.3M the week of May 18). Indie and accidental movements are NOT explained by fewer users — the base grew.

blocks.caveat: Refreshed 2026-06-08. Jun 1 is now a complete week; Jun 8 is partial (1 day). May 18 full week peaked at ~1.3M devices. Note IDENTIFIES distinct USER_IDs (multi-device users) actually exceeds distinct ANONYMOUS_IDs — that's normal.

blocks.title: ★ CS data · accidental indy self-signup cancellations
blocks.subtitle: Direct signal from the CS team. Cancellations tagged Internal Reason = 'Accidental Indy Self-Signup'. Fresh export (47 records since Apr 1) replaces the premature May 11 pull.

preBaseline.label: By signup week, mature data (late Mar - mid May)

comparison.verdict: CORRECTED: the experiment is FLAT on accidental signups, not a win. By signup week the rate is a steady ~3-5/wk before and during the experiment (May 4/11/18 = 3/3/4 — no May 18 spike). The old '0 in May' was a premature partial pull and is dead. By cancellation month the 47 records land Apr 28 / May 16 / Jun 3 (cancellations lag signups, so the most recent signup weeks are still filling in). Don't claim Flow B reduces accidental signups; lead on friction + welcome reach + the email-step duplicate catch.

blocks.source: McChesney Matro · CS team · 'Indy Cancellations This Month' export as of 2026-06-03 14:32 EST · filtered to Internal Reason = Accidental Indy Self-Signup · 47 records since Apr 1 (counts above are by cancellation month)
blocks.caveat: The export is pre-filtered to accidental-tagged cancellations, so 'total' = accidental here (100%). Counts above are by CANCELLATION month (Apr 28 / May 16 / Jun 3); cancellations lag the signup, so June is partial and recent signup weeks under-count. The honest read is by SIGNUP week: steady ~3-5/wk through the experiment, no May 18 spike (the proxy's mid-May spike was the inc-264 abuse incident). ~11 records have May signup dates — the old '0 in May' was wrong.

now: CS re-pull DONE (export through Jun 3): 47 accidental-indy cancellations since Apr 1, steady ~3-5/wk by signup week. Correct the narrative everywhere — the experiment is FLAT on accidental signups, NOT a win. Lead on friction + welcome reach + email-step duplicate catch.
Scope leak crept to 8 devices (3 → 7 → 8) — get the version breakdown from iOS (stale 3.23.0 installs vs 3.23.1 regression).
Duplicate-email rate softening back toward baseline (20.2 → 18.8 → 16.4% vs 15.7%) — consistent with the inc-264 incident driving the mid-May spike; keep watching.
Review Clarity session recordings with Tristan — focus on email-step abandons and Flow A forgot-password taps (misclick analysis in deck 27 backs this).

Re-set the success metric with Eric: accidental signups are flat, so the Flow B case rests on friction reduction + welcome reach + catching duplicates at the email step, not an accidental-signup drop.
later: Fix the NEW-device cohort definition: the rolling 90-day window misclassifies dormant pre-experiment devices as NEW — split drifted then held (50.8/49.2 → 52.6/47.4 → 53.8/46.2 → 53.6/46.4 → 53.7/46.3, p<0.0001). Anchor the cohort to first-ever IDENTIFIES >= May 7.
Honest-narrative note: the 'indie share dropped' read INVERTED (now 3.2% vs 2.8% baseline) — drop it from any deck; the email-step duplicate catch + friction wins are the defensible signals.
Continue weekly refresh as more experiment weeks accrue; watch whether the duplicate rate fully returns to baseline.
Pair the Clarity findings with the abandons followup data once Tristan has reviewed sessions.

resolvedThisSession: Indie identifier: role='barber' AND user_type IS NULL. Zero indies in the experiment population.
temp_password ≠ indie: all temp_password Flow B users are confirmed Rental/Commission shop barbers.
SRM 73/27 root cause: sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. New post-100% devices split 59/41 (close to expected). Not a bucketing bug.
flowB 3% logged-in rate explained: iOS auth flow fires only at session start (cold start / sign-in / context-switch / temp deep link). flowB skews to new anonymous devices.
Indie north-star is unmeasurable in this experiment: indies have a separate self-onboarding flow (*_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events) that the iOS login experiment doesn't touch.
Flow A frustration tapping is real and persistent (12-24 taps/user) — not a legacy artifact, concentrated in 5-9 users per period.
temp_password steady-state on clean data is ~27% — the apparent 33.5% → 23% drop layered two effects: sample contamination + invited-barber backlog clearing.
SRM resolved on clean NEW data: 50.7/49.3 as of 2026-05-12 — confirms the 73/27 was sticky-bucket artifact, not a bucketing flaw.

Accidental indies (indies whose email matches a Rental/Commission user) dropped ~65% vs an 8-week pre-experiment baseline — sharpest in-Snowflake signal that Flow B is reducing duplicates specifically.
